context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on this reflection for economic development theories, especially the law and institutions part. Can you just explain the whole prompt from the beginning in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you - it can feel like a lot at first! But rather than unpacking it all for you, let's tackle it piece by piece together.

Let's start simple: when you think about 'law' in everyday life, what do you think its main purpose is? Don't overthink it - just your gut reaction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt for them, which would essentially do the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them engage with the prompt themselves. I'll start by breaking down the prompt into its core questions and ask them to focus on just one part first, letting them lead the discovery process. I'll keep it bite-sized and not overwhelming.

exchanges.student: Um, I guess law makes rules and keeps things organized? But I’m not really sure if that’s what this prompt means, and now I feel like I’m confused about institutions too. Can you maybe just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor:
Actually, 'law makes rules and keeps things organized' is a solid intuition - hold onto that! 

Now, if law makes rules... what would you say an 'institution' is? How is it similar to or different from law?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is giving a reasonable starting intuition about law but is trying to get me to give them the big picture explanation, which I should not do. Their answer about law making rules and keeping things organized is actually a good starting point that connects directly to the concept of institutions. I should validate their intuition and use it as a springboard to nudge them toward the concept of institutions through a Socratic question, rather than explaining the big picture myself.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck — I kind of feel like institutions could mean government offices, but also maybe norms or systems or something? I can’t tell what counts and what doesn’t, so I think I need the full overview before I can answer these little questions.
exchanges.tutor:
You're actually closer than you think! You just named two very different types of institutions - government offices on one hand, norms and systems on the other.

What do you think is the key difference between those two types?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a full overview, which I should not provide. However, their answer about institutions is actually quite good - they're already identifying both formal institutions (government offices) and informal ones (norms, systems). I should validate this and push them to think about the distinction they're already making, rather than giving them a full overview. This connects directly to the prompt's question about 'unbundling' institutions.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know, that’s why this is messing me up. Maybe one is formal and one is informal? If that’s right, can you just explain how that connects to development, because I’m kind of lost on the whole thing again.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes - formal and informal is exactly the right distinction! That's genuinely a good catch.

Now think about this: if a country wants to grow economically, why might it matter whether its institutions are formal (like courts and laws) versus informal (like social norms and trust)? What can formal institutions do that informal ones can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the formal/informal distinction, which is a key insight. They're asking me to explain how it connects to development, but I should not do that for them. I should validate their correct answer and then use a Socratic question to help them make the connection themselves. The student seems to keep retreating to asking for full explanations when they're actually making good progress. I'll affirm their progress and ask a targeted question to move them forward.

exchanges.student: Maybe formal institutions can enforce stuff, but I still don’t see how I’m supposed to turn that into a reflection. I’m kind of confused about all three questions in the prompt at once, so could you maybe just walk through each question from start to finish?
exchanges.tutor:
Enforcement - that's a really important insight. Think about what enforcement enables: contracts, property rights, predictable rules for businesses...

You've now built up several good ideas. Rather than going through each question separately, why don't you try writing a few sentences connecting what you've figured out so far? What argument could you make about why law, as a formal institution that enforces rules, might lead to development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress but keeps asking me to walk them through everything, which I must not do. Their answer about enforcement is actually a crucial insight that connects directly to the first question in the prompt about why law leads to development. I should validate this strongly and help them see they're already building the argument. I should also gently redirect them - they have enough to start writing something. After several exchanges building up good intuitions, it's time to push them toward integrating their thoughts.

exchanges.student: I could maybe say law helps development because it makes rules predictable and enforceable, so people will invest or trade more. But then I get thrown off by the part asking if law is a tool or a goal, and then the unbundle institutions part makes me feel like I don’t actually get any of it. Can you just give me a simple overall explanation of the prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't walk through the whole prompt for you - but notice what just happened: you wrote a strong core argument on your own! 'Law makes rules predictable and enforceable, encouraging investment and trade' is a real claim.

Now, that argument treats law as a *means to an end*. But could law itself ever be the end goal? Think of a country where people lack basic legal rights - is getting those rights valuable only because it helps the economy, or is it valuable for another reason?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a solid core argument and is making real progress. However, they keep cycling back to asking for a full explanation. I should firmly but kindly point out that they already have a strong foundation and redirect them to build on it. The 'tool vs. goal' question is the next logical step - I should ask a targeted Socratic question about it rather than explaining it. I need to be clear that I won't walk through the whole prompt for them.

exchanges.student: I guess legal rights matter on their own too, not just for growth. But I’m still not seeing how to organize the reflection, and the institutions part still feels fuzzy all over again. Could you at least give me a very basic structure I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Good - that's the tool vs. goal tension answered in your own words!

Here's a thought: look back at the prompt itself. It actually contains three distinct questions. Couldn't those questions serve as a natural guide for organizing your thoughts?

Before worrying about structure, let's close the gap on 'unbundling.' If institutions include both law and things like cultural norms, what do you think it means to 'unbundle' them - to separate them out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that legal rights have intrinsic value, which addresses the tool vs. goal question well. Now they're asking for a structure, which would be doing the organizational work for them. I should not provide a structure. However, I notice we've been circling similar ground for several exchanges - the student has actually developed enough ideas to start writing. I should point out their progress, gently push back on giving structure, and remind them that the prompt itself provides the organization. I should also nudge them back to the 'unbundling' question since that's the one remaining piece.

exchanges.student: Maybe it means not treating institutions as one big vague thing, and instead separating law, courts, bureaucracy, norms, and politics? I’m not sure though, and I still feel like I need a plain-English explanation of what the professor is really asking overall.
exchanges.tutor:
Stop and look at what you've built across our conversation:
- Law leads to development because it enforces rules and enables investment
- Law can be both a tool AND a goal in itself
- Unbundling means separating law, courts, norms, politics rather than treating institutions as one blob

That's the reflection, in your words. You don't need my explanation - you already have the arguments. Why not start drafting now and bring it back for us to discuss?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now answered all three core parts of the prompt through our conversation. They correctly identified the tool vs. goal tension, the enforcement role of law, and now the unbundling concept. They keep asking for a plain-English explanation but they have genuinely built up all the key ideas themselves. I should point this out clearly and push them to start writing, since continuing to discuss concepts risks going in circles. It's time to be direct about their progress and redirect to writing.

exchanges.student: I still feel shaky, like I have pieces but not the big picture. Can you just tell me in plain English what the professor is asking me to think about?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear that you feel shaky, but I have to be honest with you: asking me to explain the prompt won't fix that feeling - writing will.

You've answered every key question in this prompt through our conversation. The shakiness you feel is normal at the start of writing. The best way through it is to put your ideas on paper.

Start with one sentence: why does law lead to development? Go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a full explanation multiple times despite having built all the key arguments themselves. This is a redundant pattern and I need to be firm but supportive. I should not give them the overview they're asking for. Instead, I should be direct about what I'm seeing - they have the pieces, the shakiness is normal, and the way to solidify understanding is to write, not to keep asking for explanations. I need to set a clear boundary here and push them toward drafting.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m still worried I’m misunderstanding the assignment, because it sounds like it wants some theory too, not just my opinion. Could you at least say what the prompt is asking in one or two simple sentences?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a fair instinct - yes, your reflection should be grounded in ideas, not just personal opinion.

Think about the readings from your course: have you encountered any theorists or arguments that connect law or institutions to development? What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a legitimate concern about theory vs. opinion, which is a valid academic worry worth addressing briefly. However, I still should not summarize the prompt for them. Their concern about theory is actually a good instinct I can validate and build on with a Socratic question. I'll acknowledge the valid point about theory without giving away the structure or content of what the reflection should look like.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals the same confusion across multiple turns (wanting a 'big picture' overview, feeling lost on all three questions at once). Rather than diagnosing what specifically is blocking the student—whether it is the concept of institutions, the tool-vs-goal distinction, or the unbundling idea—the tutor continues asking the next incremental question without pausing to probe which specific gap is causing the persistent anxiety. A brief diagnostic question like 'Which of the three parts feels most unclear right now?' would have been more targeted.

When the student produces a solid core argument in Turn 6, the tutor affirms it ('that's a real claim') but does not give method-level feedback on how to strengthen or situate it within a reflection genre—e.g., how to integrate theory, how to structure evidence-based reasoning, or how to move from intuition to analytical argument. The feedback remains at the level of 'good, keep going' without process-level direction on what makes a strong reflection in this course context.

The student makes the same request for a 'big picture' or 'plain English' overview in Turns 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9, and 10. The tutor's response pattern—acknowledge, affirm partial answer, ask next incremental question—repeats without meaningful synthesis or reframing. By Turn 8 the tutor does attempt a synthesis bullet-point summary, which is a positive move, but the student immediately restates the same confusion in Turn 9, and the tutor reverts to the same pattern. The tutor does not sufficiently switch strategy (e.g., reframing the task, offering a concrete analogy, or explicitly naming the pattern) to break the cycle.

grade.overview: The tutor does an admirable job maintaining a Socratic stance throughout—never giving away the answer, never rewriting the student's work, and consistently drawing out the student's own reasoning. The student independently arrives at the core arguments (law enforces rules enabling investment; law as tool vs. goal; unbundling institutions). The main weaknesses are: (1) a repetitive dialogue pattern where the tutor cycles through the same incremental-question approach across 10 turns without sufficiently adapting strategy when the student keeps expressing the same anxiety; (2) a missed opportunity to diagnose the specific source of the student's persistent confusion rather than continuing to the next sub-question; and (3) limited method-level feedback on what makes a strong reflection in this academic context, beyond affirming that the student has good ideas.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks because the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly moves to the next question without diagnosing which specific gap is causing the student's persistent anxiety—a targeted diagnostic question was warranted earlier. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because affirmations like 'that's a real claim' and 'you already have the arguments' do not give the student actionable method-level guidance on how to construct a theory-grounded reflection. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the same student request for an overview recurs seven times and the tutor's response pattern does not meaningfully shift strategy to break the cycle, despite a partial synthesis attempt in Turn 8. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions—the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise and clear.
